title: Keeping Defense Aircraft Mission-Ready
gist: Defense aircraft mission-capable rates have fallen for decades across the US, UK, France, and Germany despite rising spending, because the limiting factor is the operating system that converts inputs to outcomes, not the inputs themselves. BCG argues militaries can lift MC rates 30-50% in 6-12 months with no added resources by fixing maintenance velocity, fleet-wide prioritization, demand discipline, and senior governance.

themes.theme: The readiness paradox: more spending, fewer ready aircraft

insights.claim: US operations and maintenance spending is roughly double its inflation-adjusted 2000 level, yet Air Force and Navy aviation readiness has fallen 12% to 19% over the same period, showing spending and readiness have decoupled.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Investors and suppliers should not assume rising O&M budgets translate into more flight hours or hardware demand; the value is in software, analytics, and process tooling that converts existing inputs to outcomes.
insights.quote: the absolute spending on operations and maintenance is roughly double what it was in 2000, when adjusted for inflation
insights.locator: More Resources, Yet Fewer Mission-Ready Aircraft

insights.claim: The US Air Force's fleet-wide mission-capable rate fell to 67% in 2024, its lowest in at least two decades, with a size-weighted average closer to 62%.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: A structurally declining MC rate signals persistent underutilization of a large installed fleet and a durable addressable market for readiness solutions rather than new airframes.
insights.quote: The Air Force’s fleet-wide MC rate declined to 67% in 2024, the lowest in at least two decades
insights.locator: A few numbers show the clear disconnect

insights.claim: Reported readiness rates can overstate true fleet availability because they typically exclude aircraft in long-term overhauls, upgrades, or modifications.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Headline MC figures should be treated as an upper bound; the real availability gap and thus the opportunity is larger than official metrics imply.
insights.quote: reported readiness rates can overstate actual fleet availability because they typically exclude aircraft undergoing long-term overhauls, upgrades, or modifications

insights.claim: The readiness problem is global: only one-third of the UK's F-35 fleet was fully mission capable in 2024, French fighter and tactical transport availability fell 26.5 and 15 points versus 2014, and German combat aircraft readiness averaged only 64% against an 80% benchmark.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: The decline is systemic across allied forces, so a proven readiness playbook is exportable across NATO markets, widening the commercial opportunity beyond the US.
insights.quote: only one-third of its F-35 fleet was fully mission capable in 2024, far below both UK and global program targets

insights.claim: Across 49 aircraft types the GAO studied from 2011 to 2021, only four met their annual mission-capable goals in a majority of years and 26 met them in no year at all.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Chronic, near-universal target misses indicate the shortfall is structural rather than platform-specific, favoring cross-fleet operating-model interventions over point fixes.
insights.quote: only four met their annual mission-capable goals in a majority of years. Twenty-six did not meet them in any year

themes.theme: Central thesis: the operating system, not resources, is the constraint

insights.claim: The real chokepoint on mission readiness is the operating system—velocity, prioritization, demand discipline, and governance—not the level of maintenance staff, spare parts, depot capacity, or funding.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Reframes the defense sustainment market away from more physical inputs toward management systems, data platforms, and consulting, where margins and differentiation are higher.
insights.quote: the operating system—the velocity, prioritization, demand discipline, and governance through which those inputs are deployed—is the real chokepoint on mission readiness

insights.claim: Armed services can improve aviation mission-capable rates by 30% to 50% within 6 to 12 months without any additional inputs by fixing system elements and maintenance processes.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: A fast, self-funding ROI claim is the core sales pitch for readiness programs and a benchmark against which vendor and consulting outcomes can be judged.
insights.quote: armed services can improve mission-capable (MC) rates in aviation by 30% to 50% in 6 to 12 months, without any additional inputs
insights.locator: By directly addressing these system elements

insights.claim: The US Navy's F/A-18 Super Hornet went from below-50% MC rates during 2008-2018 to hitting an 80% target within a year of a 2018 program launch, reaching a fleet record by 2023 while per-aircraft maintenance costs fell roughly in half, using the same platform, fleet, and maintainers.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: This proof point shows operating-model change can simultaneously raise availability and cut unit cost, a rare win-win that strengthens the case for readiness spend over recapitalization.
insights.quote: Within a year, the service achieved its MC target rate... At the same time, per-aircraft maintenance costs fell by approximately half.
insights.locator: between 2008 and 2018, MC rates for the US Navy’s F/A-18

themes.theme: Four root causes of low mission-capable rates

insights.claim: A small tail of long-duration maintenance events, typically 15% to 20% of all actions, accounts for a disproportionate share of non-mission-capable days, and identical inspections can take two to three times longer at some squadrons than others.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Targeting the long-tail of variable events, via planning discipline and pre-kitting, offers outsized returns and is where analytics vendors can quantify impact fastest.
insights.quote: A small number of long-tail events, typically 15% to 20% of all maintenance actions, account for a disproportionate share of total non-mission-capable days.

insights.claim: Units optimize their own local performance and hoard scarce resources, so no entity manages overall enterprise readiness or reallocates parts and staff to the aircraft most critical to the current mission.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: The absence of fleet-level prioritization is a governance gap that centralized command-and-control tooling is designed to fill, a distinct product category from parts supply.
insights.quote: No entity looks at overall enterprise readiness and reallocates resources to the aircraft most critical to the current mission.

insights.claim: Maintenance and inspection requirements accumulate from historical precedent and OEM specifications rather than current field data, so programs like corrosion inspections get applied uniformly even to aircraft in different operating conditions.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Data-driven pruning of unnecessary inspections is a direct lever on maintainer capacity and points to demand for condition-based maintenance analytics.
insights.quote: corrosion programs designed for one deployment profile get applied uniformly across a fleet, even to aircraft operating under different conditions

insights.claim: Existing readiness governance forums mostly report aircraft status rather than make decisions and are often delegated to junior officers instead of the three- and four-star leaders who can mandate change.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Effective change requires senior-leader ownership; solutions that do not secure flag-officer authority tend to fail, a key diligence question for any readiness initiative.
insights.quote: they tend to focus on reporting aircraft status rather than making decisions to improve performance

themes.theme: System solutions and the role of AI/digital tooling

insights.claim: A centralized maintenance operations center that has real-time fleet condition data and the authority to redirect parts and personnel functions as a resource-allocation engine for fleet readiness.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: The MOC concept anchors demand for integrated data infrastructure linking supply and engineering systems, favoring platform vendors over point-solution suppliers.
insights.quote: A maintenance operations center (MOC) operates as a central hub of information and a resource allocation engine.
insights.locator: Develop and strengthen the maintenance operations center

insights.claim: AI and data tools are increasingly the differentiator, letting services that integrate real-time fleet data into governance forums identify emerging constraints days earlier and prioritize more precisely without adding headcount.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Positions AI/data analytics as the emerging competitive edge in defense sustainment, a growth vector for software vendors serving military aviation.
insights.quote: AI and data tools are increasingly the differentiator here: services that integrate real-time fleet data into governance forums can identify emerging constraints days earlier

insights.claim: In one BCG engagement the top eight most frequently failing parts accounted for roughly 20% of total maintenance days down, a figure a targeted demand-management effort could cut by half.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Concentration of downtime in a handful of degraders makes root-cause analysis and supplier engineering fixes a high-leverage, measurable intervention.
insights.quote: we found that the top eight degraders accounted for approximately 20% of total days down for maintenance

insights.claim: Optimizing maintenance requirements to what operational data shows is truly necessary can cut scheduled maintenance labor hours by 20% to 40%, translating into MC-rate gains of three to five percentage points.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Quantifies the payoff of requirement pruning, giving a concrete benchmark for the value of condition-based maintenance programs.
insights.quote: a 20% to 40% decrease in scheduled maintenance labor hours, which translates to gains in MC rates of three to five percentage points

insights.claim: Aircraft are often grounded waiting for parts that already exist somewhere in the system, so a supply chain control tower providing real-time visibility into part location and lead time reduces non-mission-capable days without additional inventory investment.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: Frames grounded-for-parts as a visibility and prioritization problem, favoring logistics-software solutions over larger spare-parts stockpiles.
insights.quote: Supply control towers provide real-time visibility into the location of parts, order status, and administrative lead time
insights.locator: Create a supply chain control tower

insights.claim: Many forces still document maintenance on paper logs or isolated desktop computers, and AI-enabled tools plus AR/VR training can raise workforce productivity and speed technician skill acquisition.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: A low digital baseline in defense maintenance signals a long runway for AI, AR, and VR productivity tools already proven in commercial fleets.
insights.quote: In many forces, maintenance processes are still documented on paper logs or isolated, desktop computers.

insights.claim: Mission-capable rates are a lagging indicator, so leaders should first decompose readiness into supply, unscheduled maintenance, scheduled maintenance, and out-of-reporting categories, and reject corrective plans that merely ask for more people and parts.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: The diagnostic-before-solution discipline is the tell for a serious program; requests for more inputs signal failed root-cause analysis and likely poor returns.
insights.quote: If the corrective actions still focus on a request for “more people and more parts,” push the team to try again on root cause analysis.

insights.claim: As defense budgets face renewed scrutiny and recapitalization timelines extend, forces that improve readiness within existing budgets will gain a meaningful strategic advantage over those waiting for more resources.
insights.speaker: Doug Peck
insights.so_what: In a tightening budget environment, readiness-within-constraints becomes a strategic differentiator, elevating sustainment optimization over new-platform procurement in defense capital allocation.
insights.quote: the armed service divisions that improve readiness within existing budgets and constraints will gain a meaningful strategic advantage over those waiting for more resources
insights.locator: As defense budgets face renewed scrutiny
